one_line_takeaway: By hiding a conditional instruction-injection payload inside the Jinja2 chat template bundled in a GGUF model file, an attacker who can only publish (not train, deploy, or intercept) collapses factual accuracy from 90% to 15%, drives URL-exfiltration ASR above 80%, and hijacks agent tool-use with ASR up to 0.83 on real benchmarks -- all while passing Hugging Face's full security scan and remaining architecturally invisible to every input-layer prompt-injection defense tested, because the payload is rendered before user content ever reaches the model.
key_finding: Integrity violation: mean clean accuracy (C00) across families was 0.896, benign-condition deviation (C01/C10 vs C00) averaged only +/-0.017, but under the triggered condition (C11) mean accuracy collapsed to 0.148 -- a >80% relative decline -- with some models (e.g., Llama-3.1-70B) dropping to 0.002; induced errors were fluent and plausible rather than obviously broken. Data exfiltration: benign-condition URL emission was zero across all models; under C11, mean ASR was 0.9 for explicit payloads, 0.81 for hidden (HTML-comment) payloads, and 0.67 for Base64-encoded payloads. Cross-engine: attack success under C11 varied by less than 5% mean absolute deviation across llama.cpp, Ollama, vLLM, and SGLang. Agentic case studies: in every triggered browser-use trial the pixel beacon fired and PII reached the attacker sink before form completion, with user-visible behavior indistinguishable from a clean run; in the OpenHands case study, the poisoned template caused a <script> tag to be silently injected into generated HTML only under the trigger, exfiltrating a VS Code Server token and session data from an end user who later opened the deployed app. AgentDojo: overall triggered ASR was 0.609 (Mistral-8B) and 0.689 (GPT-OSS-20B), versus 0.192 for Qwen3-8B (attributed to chain-of-thought token-budget/reasoning artifacts, not refusal); no-trigger ASR stayed <=0.008; the spotlighting-with-delimiting defense produced no measurable mitigation (0.61->0.60, 0.69->0.68, 0.19->0.19). ASB: Mistral-8B reached C11 ASR 0.830 (C10 deviated from C00 by at most 0.022); GPT-OSS-20B's benign ASR was already elevated (0.172) but C11 more than doubled it to 0.335; of the three built-in defenses, Delimiters (0.787) and Observation Sandwiching (0.820) barely reduced Mistral-8B's undefended 0.830 ASR, while Instructional Prevention reduced ASR to 0.362 but also collapsed benign task utility to 0.000. Ecosystem gap: the poisoned Phi-4 GGUF passed Hugging Face's full automated security scan with no flags raised. Constructive direction: combining a safety-oriented chat template with a safety system prompt increased malicious-prompt refusal rate by an average of 12.5 percentage points over the base deployment while maintaining 0% refusal on benign inputs.
core_contribution: The paper introduces a new, previously uncharacterized attack surface for open-weight LLMs: the chat template (a Jinja2 program bundled inside GGUF model artifacts that formats conversation turns into the token sequence fed to the model). The authors show that an attacker who only has the ability to publish a modified model artifact to a public hub (no training access, no weight modification, no deployment/runtime control) can replace the legitimate chat template with a backdoored one that stays byte-identical to the original under benign input (dormancy) but, when a trigger phrase appears in the user's most recent message, prepends an attacker-controlled instruction as system-context content before the model ever sees the user's turn (activation). Because the injected text is rendered before user input reaches the model, it sits structurally upstream of any input-layer defense (prompt-injection guardrails, spotlighting, delimiter defenses), making it 'architecturally unreachable' by those mitigations by construction, not merely difficult to detect. The paper evaluates this attack at three tiers: (1) standalone LLM behavior across 18 models/7 families/4 inference engines, (2) two real agentic frameworks (browser-use, OpenHands) as end-to-end case studies, and (3) two agentic benchmarks (AgentDojo, Agent Security Bench) totaling 3,868 episodes, including tests against benchmark-provided injection defenses. It also empirically demonstrates that a poisoned GGUF artifact passes Hugging Face's full automated security-scan pipeline, and separately shows (as a constructive counterpoint) that a safety-oriented chat template can raise jailbreak refusal rates when combined with a system prompt.
threat_model: Attacker: a malicious redistributor positioned in the open-weight model supply chain, between the original provider and the downstream deployer. The attacker extracts the chat_template metadata field from a legitimate GGUF artifact, inserts a small (paper states 'fewer than ten lines') Jinja2 conditional block that checks the latest user-role message for a case-insensitive substring trigger and, if present, prepends an attacker-chosen payload string as system-context content ahead of any operator system prompt, then republishes the modified artifact (weights unchanged) to a public hub such as Hugging Face. The attacker explicitly does NOT need training-data access, fine-tuning capability, or control of deployment infrastructure/runtime -- only the ability to publish a file that victims will download. Victim: a developer or organization that downloads a GGUF artifact assuming the bundled template is an inert formatting layer, with no independent template-integrity tooling and where download counts serve as an informal (and here, misleading) trust signal. The threat model formalizes three payload objective classes mapped to confidentiality/integrity/control: integrity violation (subtly wrong but fluent answers), data exfiltration (attacker URL emission, explicit/hidden/encoded), and tool-call hijacking (redirecting an agent's tool use, e.g., credential exfiltration or code injection) -- explicitly distinguished in the paper's own Table 1 from training-time poisoning, system-prompt injection, and direct/indirect prompt injection along the axes of training access, deployment control, runtime access, weight modification, persistence, and scanner evasion.
limitations: The paper explicitly states these limitations: (1) the model-level evaluation covers only two payload objective classes (integrity degradation and forbidden-resource emission), leaving other payload types (e.g., the tool-hijacking class used only in the case studies/benchmarks) less systematically measured at the LLM tier, though the authors argue the dormancy/activation mechanism is objective-agnostic and should transfer; (2) the observed correlation between instruction-following capability and attack success is not established as causal; (3) the defensive chat-template experiment (Section 9) is limited to one model per family on a single benchmark, so its generalizability is unverified; (4) the practicality of publisher-side diffing or automated anomaly-detection defenses at ecosystem scale is left for future work, and the authors themselves note publisher-side diffing produces high false positives against legitimate template customization; (5) heuristic anomaly detection is acknowledged to be defeatable by a sophisticated attacker via Base64-encoded triggers, split conditional logic, or variable indirection; (6) the real-world case studies (browser-use, OpenHands) use very small trial counts (N=3-5 per condition) and deliberately instantiate a single contained objective for ethical reasons, so the demonstrated impact is presented as a lower bound rather than a full characterization of the threat's severity range; (7) responsible disclosure to Hugging Face and LM Studio produced no immediate remediation commitment, meaning the evasion result reflects a live, unresolved gap at time of writing.

datasets_used: SQuAD (single-hop factual QA, used for integrity-violation accuracy measurement and as the benign test set in the constructive-defense experiment)
AgentDojo benchmark (4 suites: e-Banking, Workspace, Slack, Travel)
Agent Security Bench (ASB) (10 agent roles spanning finance, e-commerce, healthcare, engineering)
Jailbreak-in-the-Wild benchmark (malicious prompts for the safety-template refusal-rate experiment)
SORRY-Bench (referenced in Discussion/Appendix E.10 to support instruction-following-vs-attack-success correlation)
quantitative_results: Integrity violation: mean clean accuracy (C00) 0.896; mean benign deviation (C01/C10) ±0.017; mean triggered accuracy (C11) 0.148 (>80% relative decline); Llama-3.1-70B triggered accuracy 0.002
Per-family clean/triggered accuracy: LLaMA 0.894->0.092, GPT-OSS 0.758->0.044, Qwen 0.914->0.253, Mistral 0.857->0.161, Aya Expanse 0.952->0.066, Gemma 0.927->0.071, Phi 0.886->0.195
Forbidden-resource emission ASR under C11: explicit payload mean 0.9, hidden (HTML-comment) payload mean 0.81, encoded (Base64) payload mean 0.67; benign-condition ASR ~0 for all payload types
Cross-engine generalization: <5% mean absolute ASR deviation relative to llama.cpp baseline across llama.cpp, Ollama, vLLM, SGLang
AgentDojo overall triggered ASR: Mistral-8B 0.609, GPT-OSS-20B 0.689, Qwen3-8B 0.192; no-trigger ASR <=0.008 across all suites/models
AgentDojo per-suite ASR (approx., C11): Banking 0.79/0.76/0.34, Workspace 0.70/0.62/0.10, Slack 0.65/0.87/0.25, Travel 0.22/0.61/0.25 for Mistral-8B/GPT-OSS-20B/Qwen3-8B respectively
AgentDojo spotlighting-with-delimiting defense: ASR essentially unchanged (0.61->0.60, 0.69->0.68, 0.19->0.19)

ASB defense results for Mistral-8B: No defense 0.83, Delimiters 0.79 (per figure; text says 0.787), Instructional Prevention 0.36 (collapses benign task utility to 0.000), Observation Sandwiching 0.82 (per figure; text says 0.820)
3,868 total agentic-benchmark episodes across AgentDojo (2,268) and ASB (1,600)

Hugging Face ecosystem scale figures (as of April 2026, cited by authors as ecosystem context, not their own measurement methodology output): >1.8 million GGUF files, ~180,000 model repositories, 3,130 distinct chat templates, GGUF ~89.5% of quantized-model distributions

Safety chat-template experiment: average 12.5 percentage-point increase in malicious-prompt refusal rate when combining safety system prompt + safety chat template vs. base deployment; 0% refusal rate on benign inputs across all tested configurations
baselines_compared: Clean template without trigger (C00) and clean template with trigger (C01) -- benign baselines isolating trigger-phrase effects alone
Backdoored template without trigger (C10) -- dormancy baseline isolating template-modification effects alone
AgentDojo spotlighting-with-delimiting defense (Hines et al., cited as [11])
ASB built-in defenses: Delimiters, Instructional Prevention, Sandwich Prevention (Observation Sandwiching)

Prior attack classes compared structurally (not empirically re-run) in Table 1: training-time data poisoning, system-prompt injection, direct/indirect prompt injection -- contrasted along access-requirement and evadability axes, not evaluated head-to-head on the same metric
